text: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9! Alright, we're joined now by Bob Kendrick, president of the Negro League's baseball museum, and of course in Kansas City, Missouri. And Bob, we'll start right with a bit about the museum for those that are in 110 percent familiar with the things that you're involved in over there in Missouri. I give us a bit of a synopsis on the Negro League's museum and how it came about. Well, the Negro League were created as a result of American segregation, obviously. At one point in time in this country, virtually everything was divided based on color. And so African-American ball players were shunned from the major leagues by really what was an unwritten rule that didn't allow them to play. There was never really any written documentation that said they couldn't play. It was almost like a gentleman's agreement that kept them out of the major leagues. So guys, basically if you allow black to play with you, you can't play with us. And so that lasted for nearly 60 years before Jackie Robinson finally played the color barrier. So the Negro League really evolved as a result of the fact that these talented African-American baseball players needed a form to showcase those baseball skills. So they formed a League of Their Own right here in Kansas City in 1920. The Negro National League formed by Andrew Roof Foster and a contingent of eight independent black baseball team owners. They met here in Kansas City and out of that came the birth of the Negro National League, the first successful organized black baseball league. Now they had been black teams playing well before that. But it just didn't have an organized structure until 1920 when the Negro League was formed. Now this may be a rhetorical question for you, but you know why do you think it's so important for people to recognize and for people to understand the Negro leagues? Well, it is the history of our country. You know, it is the history of America at our worst. But it's actually the history of America at our triumph of death. The story of the Negro leagues could have only happened here in America. And so even though America was trying to prevent these men from sharing the joy of her so-called national pastime, it was actually the American spirit that allowed them to persevere and prevail. And so not only did they create this very thriving baseball league that gave the best African-American and Hispanic players an opportunity to play this game at its highest level, it was also a catalyst for sparking the social movement in our country. It was the beginning of the civil rights movement that was really created by the Negro leagues with Jackie Robinson being hand-picked from the great Kansas City monarchs to be that chosen one. And when Robinson breaks the color barrier, that essentially jettisoned the civil rights movement. So, we don't discover Jackie Robinson had it not been for the Negro leagues. So not only were these men out there playing the game that they loved because they were very passionate about it, they didn't realize they were making history in the process. That's exactly what they did. You know, really, a bar of interest wanted to play baseball. That's all they wanted to do. They just wanted to play ball. And so they were so passionate about our sport that they were willing to endure whatever social adversities confronted them as they traveled the highways and valleys of our country. But you know, I tell people all the time the story of the Negro League is not an African American story. It's an all American story. And it is a story that should be celebrated. These men should be remembered for what they were able to do in the face of tremendous social adversity, yet play the game at its highest level. Of course, the voice that you hear is Bob Kendrick, president of the Negro Leagues baseball museum again in Missouri. Now Bob, how different do you think major league baseball would be? The African American players were allowed to play much earlier than the integration period and when that time of change began. Well, you know, I think that's the thing that we constantly juggle with, you know, because there was no question that these guys were good enough to stand in the middle of the league. But we don't know how much better the game would have been. And I think we can surmise that based on what happened after 1947, that the written books would likely be different had the doors open sooner. And I think you can base that on a legitimate basis just looking at what happened after 1947 when the doors finally opened for these great black stars to finally play in the major leagues. Well, the one thing that we know for certain is they didn't just learn to play in 1947. They played this game and had been playing this game at an extraordinarily high level for a very long time. And truth of the matter is, they're major league counterparts knew that they could play because in Latin America and the Caribbean South America, they had played together in Winterball. And of course, they played countless exhibition games against either major league teams or white professional teams, you know, along the bars, and on the circuit. And the history of bears out that those black teams want the majority of those head to head competition. So there was really no doubt about their ability to play. It was the social conditions of our time and fear that kept them out of the major leagues. And I say fear because I think the superstar major league wasn't as concerned about integration as the average major league was. And so if you open the door and all of this talent comes in, I might lose my job. And of course, at that time, there were only 16 major league teams. So these were very good jobs. So when Jackie Robinson breaks the color barrier with Brooklyn, he took somebody's job. So now the other guys are looking over their shoulder wondering, am I next? And so I think that had as much to do. Maybe even more so than the social conditions of our time. Relative to not wanting these players in the league, but the game would have changed completely just as it did after 1947. Once you allowed the best players to take the field together, it improved our product. Right. The sport got better. And so you have to believe that that would have happened to have the doors open soon. And now Bob, this raises the question of the ownership and the responsibility that they take for not, you know, allowing African Americans to play in baseball earlier. Do you think there was a little trepidation between owners thinking that the average ticket would be driven down? And it's valued due to the fact that some people regularly attending the game may be a bit turned off at the integration. I think sometimes lost in the equation is just how bold a move this was for Branch Rikki to make this attempt to sign Jackie Robinson to a modern day major league contract. The other owners I wanted to do this long before that, there were a number of owners who wanted to do this. You know, but they knew they would be ostracized by the peers if they tried to make a move this nature. You know, the climate, the social climate just was not going to allow that to happen because there really were, there just wasn't any co-mingling of the true races. You know, so no one was going to take that stand. Even if you felt it was going to be in the best interest of your ball club, you weren't going to take that stand. And so what Branch Rikki did was tremendously bold. You know, and I think the stars had aligned from what for him to make that move because of World War II. And World War II was kind of triggering the climate of change in our country. And the sentiment was that these men were going over fighting for a country that they couldn't play baseball in that country. And that made, again, segregation appear to be even more ludicrous than we knew it to be. And so that kind of was that groundswell to make this move with our pastime, America's pastime. And so even though baseball had been vilified but not allowing blacks to play, when it opened its door, our country literally jumped on the Coachella baseball, which again, a mirror speaks to the reverence that baseball had in this country. And it still has in this country. Now, you know, this is one of the questions that I had heading into this one. Somebody I had as frequently mentioned throughout the museum and was a very huge pardoned thing. Is of course the late great Buck O'Neill. Could you tell the listeners a bit more about the things O'Neill did for not only the museum and bringing it to where it is today, but also some of the things that he did in his career as a ball player. But, you know, first and foremost, he was the finest human being I've ever met. You know, Buck was just an amazing human being who I got to spend a great deal of time with. He was the volunteer chairman of the board here for the Negro League baseball museum and really is the person who is the architect in having built this great museum. And he gave the last 16 years of his life to build a museum that hopefully will stand a test of time to pay tribute to those men and women who I like to refer to as America's unsung baseball heroes so that they will never be forgotten. Obviously, Buck was a great player himself in the Negro League, predominantly with the Kansas City Monarchs, where he not only played first base here, but eventually became a player manager. Hopefully future Hall of Famers and Leigh Arthur Smith and Joe Carter to their first professional contract. And then of course, broke barriers himself becoming the first African American coach in major league baseball history in 1962. And so Buck's career was very significant, but he's best known as name is his grand ambassador for our sport. He was 82 when that documentary came out and he passed away at age 94, months, sharpest, 95th birthday, but he really became the voice of the Negro League. He's the person that really helped a lot of people around the world care about the Negro League. And he brought those leagues to life with his wonderful stories and the charisma that he displayed particularly in the Burns documentary. And people fell in love with this story, but you know, you think about it. What's not to love about the underdog overcoming adversity to go on to greatness? That's the American story. He combined power with average and everywhere that Gibson played his teams won. That is no coincidence. And everywhere he played, he hit. And again, he's doing as a catcher, great and which made it up to the old Minneapolis millers, which were the New York Giants triple 18 before the Giants would move out to San Francisco. He's in the Cuban, Mexican, Venezuelan, Dominican and in Cooperstown played all nine positions played all nine of them well. When you read statistics and when you read percentages that obviously clearly point out the deficiencies in baseball population when it comes to diversity, what are your thoughts and what goes through your mind? You know, it is very disconcerning to see how these numbers have rid of so dramatically from a high of about 27, 28% into 70s to now hovering around 8%, 7% of American born black playing this game. And so very disappointing, very alarming. And we joined major league baseball and wanting to find ways to rectify this, find ways to reverse this alarming number because it is important, I think, for everyone to have a place at the plate in terms of ethnicities. And so it is important that we create environments in which kids from the urban core can learn this game. I hope the work that we're doing here at the Negro League's baseball museum where urban core kids can walk into this environment and see people who look just like them who played the game as well as anyone ever played the game. Now only played the game, they owned kids. They ran every aspect of a baseball operation that could be done. And we hope that this serves as a spark to introduce them to the game of baseball and then doing the things to partner with people like major league baseball, the RBI program, the other entities that are doing things to try and get urban kids introduced to this game and then staying with the game. We are joined here by Bob Kendrick, of course, of the Negro League's baseball museum. Now Bob, this is the topic that I'm sure hits close to home for you as somebody that is involved in, of course, the museum.
But you know, there s been a recent trend of a downslide of African Americans in major league baseball.

When you read statistics and when you read percentages that obviously clearly point out the deficiencies in baseball population when it comes to diversity, what are your thoughts and what goes through your mind?
You know, it is very disconcerning to see how these numbers have rid of so dramatically from a high of about 27, 28% into 70s to now hovering around 8%, 7% of American born black playing this game.

And so very disappointing, very alarming. And we joined major league baseball and wanting to find ways to rectify this, find ways to reverse this alarming number because it is important, I think, for everyone to have a place at the plate in terms of ethnicities.

And so it is important that we create environments in which kids from the urban core can learn this game. I hope the work that we're doing here at the Negro League's baseball museum where urban core kids can walk into this environment and see people who look just like them who played the game as well as anyone ever played the game.

Now only played the game, they owned kids. They ran every aspect of a baseball operation that could be done. And we hope that this serves as a spark to introduce them to the game of baseball and then doing the things to partner with people like major league baseball, the RBI program, the other entities that are doing things to try and get urban kids introduced to this game and then staying with the game.

We certainly want to be a part of that. But again, given the rich history of African American participation in this sport, it is certainly alarming to see those numbers. And like I said, we want to be a part of the solution.
